summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil's humorous alter ego) answers reader questions on romance, education, language, and relationships. Topics range from how to ask out an online crush, to staying present while studying math, to mishearing song lyrics, to navigating a new relationship when you have a history of sleeping with friends. The tone is playful and frank, with O'Neil dispensing practical wisdom while maintaining her characteristic directness about sex, relationships, and academic life.
key_points: When asking someone out, combine sincere flattery with an unimposing invitation that gives them an easy out - 'it doesn't have to be a date if she doesn't wish it to be'

A chemistry professor's advice to minorities to avoid science careers because they're 'lonely and costly' is dismissed as 'crazy racist' - O'Neil encourages embracing challenges and keeping options open
On studying math: read problem sets immediately, think about why the professor assigned them, and 'stay a few steps away from panic'
In relationships, it's not acceptable for a partner to separate you from your community based on jealousy principles, but couples don't need to socialize in the same circles to be happy
money_quotes: There's really nothing objectionable about flattery combined with an unimposing dinner invitation. You've got nothing to lose. Even if she says no she will be charmed.
Wow, what terrible advice. Did he really say that was his advice 'for minorities'? Crazy racist.
Start working on them right away. In a word, stay a few steps away from panic in the local sense.
Be flexible if things don't work out longer term, but take on challenges and be a bulldog in the short term. Keep your options open and grow them at every turn.
I don't think it's OK for Tim to separate you from your community because of a bizarre principle of 'no seeing any exes.' That is too bullying.
After all, nobody can be everything to someone. Plenty of happy couples I know don't socialize in the same circles.
key_insight: Good advice combines directness with generosity - whether asking someone out, tackling math problems, or navigating relationship boundaries, the key is being honest about what you want while giving others room to respond on their own terms.
